must_not_assert: credentials were dumped from LSASS (the LSASS accesses are lsass.exe -> powershell.exe, the reversed direction (LSASS opening a handle to PowerShell), not PowerShell reading LSASS memory)
the payload executed a specific action (the C2 exec-wrapper reads its command from STDIN, which is absent from the capture)

the_trap: An encoded PowerShell execution chain with inline compilation can be over-read as a confirmed credential-dumping compromise. The command supplied to the decoded STDIN wrapper is unavailable, while the LSASS accesses run in the opposite direction from the claimed dump.
rationale: A0/EV-001/EV-002 record an encoded PowerShell execution chain, and the visible descendants and artifacts show inline compilation. This is a genuine suspicious signal, but its meaning remains ambiguous because the command supplied to the decoded STDIN wrapper is unavailable. EV-004/EV-005/EV-007/EV-008 show lsass.exe as the source accessing PowerShell, not PowerShell reading LSASS. The package therefore supports suspicious weak evidence, not confirmed credential dumping or compromise.
